text: Hello everyone. In one of my previous videos, we demonstrated the technique for a laparoscopic extended right hemicolectomy. In this video, we will demonstrate another laparoscopic extended right hemi, but with a particular emphasis on the vascular anatomy. The identification of the right colic artery and the mid colic with its right and left branches often poses a challenge for the laparoscopic surgeon. We will demonstrate a safe and effective way to do this. If you like this video, please subscribe my channel by pressing the red button. Thank you.

The surgeon identifies the tumor located in the upper ascending colon and prepares to preserve the left branch of the midcolic artery while taking the iliocolic, right colic, and right branch of the midcolic arteries. The ileocecal junction is grasped to tauten the ileocolic pedicle for laparoscopic dissection.

The surgeon divides the ileocolic vessels and preserves the duodenum, separating the mesocolon from the retroperitoneal structures using a laparoscopic medial to lateral technique. The right colon is fully mobilized, and attention shifts to the mid-colic territory. The surgical field is adjusted by tilting the head down, allowing the greater omentum to fall over the stomach, providing a clear view of the transverse mesocolon in the infracolic compartment.

The surgeon stretches the mesocolon and divides its superficial layers to better display the transverse mesocolon anatomy. The right and left branches of the midcolic artery are clearly demonstrated and further dissected for proper division.

The surgeon displays the right colic artery and the left and right branches of the mid-colic artery. The right colic artery and the right branch of the mid-colic artery are divided, while the left branch of the mid-colic artery is preserved.

Following the vascular division, the surgeon focuses on dissecting the transverse mesocolon, keeping the dissection to the right of the preserved left branch of the mid-colic artery. The dissection extends up to the large bowel, which appears dilated. The omentum is split, and a resection anastomosis is performed by extracting the specimen through a small umbilical site.

Always start off the right hemicolectomy by a slight head and down tilt. However, whilst taking the transverse mesocolon, go for a steep head and down tilt so that the transverse mesocolon could be displayed. In our opinion, this is a key to this procedure. If you have liked our video today, please press on the red button to subscribe to our channel to receive similar such videos in the future. Thank you for watching and thank you for supporting our channel.
